blocks:
Ayahs

إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ فِى لَيْلَةِ ٱلْقَدْرِ (١)
Indeed We sent it down on the Night of Ordainment. (1)
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا لَيْلَةُ ٱلْقَدْرِ (٢)
And what will show you what is the Night of Ordainment? (2)
لَيْلَةُ ٱلْقَدْرِ خَيْرٌ مِّنْ أَلْفِ شَهْرٍ (٣)
The Night of Ordainment is better than a thousand months. (3)
تَنَزَّلُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ وَٱلرُّوحُ فِيهَا بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِم مِّن كُلِّ أَمْرٍ (٤)
In it the angels and the Spirit descend, by the leave of their Lord, with every command. (4)
سَلَـٰمٌ هِىَ حَتَّىٰ مَطْلَعِ ٱلْفَجْرِ (٥)
It is peaceful until the rising of the dawn. (5)

Content and Virtue of Surah al-Qadr

The content of this Surah, as is evident from its name, pertains to the revelation of the Holy Quran on the Night of Power (Laylat al-Qadr), followed by an explanation of the significance of the Night of Power and its blessings and effects.

Regarding whether this Surah was revealed in "Mecca" or "Medina," the popular view among commentators is that it is "Meccan." However, some have suggested the possibility of it being "Medinan," as a narration states that the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله saw in a dream that the "Umayyads" were climbing his pulpit. This distressed the Prophet and made him uneasy; subsequently, Surah al-Qadr was revealed to console him (hence, some consider the verse "The Night of Power is better than a thousand months" to refer to the duration of the Umayyad rule, which lasted about a thousand months). We know that the mosque and the pulpit were established in Medina, not Mecca ^1. Nevertheless, as mentioned, the well-known view is that it is "Meccan," and this narration might be a case of "application" (tatbiq) rather than the specific "occasion of revelation" (shan al-nuzul).

Regarding the virtue of reciting this Surah, it suffices to mention what is narrated from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, who said:

"Whoever recites it will be given the reward of one who fasted the month of Ramadan and spent the Night of Power in vigil (ihya)." [^2]

In another Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read:

"Whoever recites 'Inna Anzalnahu' loudly and openly is like one who has unsheathed his sword in the way of Allah and performs Jihad; and whoever recites it secretly is like one who is drenched in his own blood in the way of Allah." ^1

It is clear that all this virtue is not for one who merely recites it without understanding its reality, but rather for the one who recites, understands, and puts its content into practice—honoring the Quran and implementing its verses in life.

^1: Ruh al-Ma'ani, vol. 30, p. 188; al-Durr al-Manthur, vol. 6, p. 371. [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, vol. 10, p. 516.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ فِي لَيْلَةِ الْقَدْرِ (1) وَ ما أَدْراكَ ما لَيْلَةُ الْقَدْرِ (2) لَيْلَةُ الْقَدْرِ خَيْرٌ مِنْ أَلْفِ شَهْرٍ (3) تَنَزَّلُ الْمَلائِكَةُ وَ الرُّوحُ فِيها بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِمْ مِنْ كُلِّ أَمْرٍ (4) سَلامٌ هِيَ حَتَّى مَطْلَعِ الْفَجْرِ (5)

Commentary

The Night of Power, the Night of the Quran's Revelation!

It is clearly derived from the verses of the Quran that the Holy Quran was revealed in the blessed month of Ramadan: "The month of Ramadan in which was revealed the Quran" (Baqarah: 185), and the apparent meaning of this expression is that the entire Quran was revealed in this month.

In the first verse of Surah al-Qadr, He adds: "Indeed, We sent it down on the Night of Power" (إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ فِي لَيْلَةِ الْقَدْرِ).

Although the name of the Quran is not explicitly mentioned in this verse, it is certain that the pronoun in "إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ" (We sent it down) refers to the Quran, and its apparent ambiguity is to emphasize its greatness and importance.

The expression "إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ" (We sent it down) is another indication of the greatness of this magnificent Heavenly Book, as Allah has attributed its revelation to Himself, especially using the first-person plural (We), which conveys a sense of majesty and grandeur.

Its revelation on the Night of "Qadr"—the night when the destinies and fates of human beings are determined—is another proof of the destiny-shaping nature of this great Heavenly Book.

By combining this verse with the verse from Surah al-Baqarah, it is concluded that the Night of Power is in the blessed month of Ramadan. But which night is it? Nothing is explicitly mentioned in the Quran in this regard, but narrations have discussed this extensively, and by Allah's will, we will speak about this and other matters at the end of the commentary of this Surah.

A question arises here: both historically and in terms of the connection between the Quran's content and the events of the Holy Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله life, it is certain that this Heavenly Book was revealed gradually over 23 years. How does this reconcile with the above verses stating it was revealed in the month of Ramadan and on the Night of Power?

The answer to this question—as many researchers have stated—is that the Quran had two types of revelation:

  1. Instantaneous Revelation (Inzal): In which the entire Quran was revealed in one night to the pure heart of the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, or to al-Bayt al-Ma'mur, or from the Preserved Tablet (al-Lawh al-Mahfuz) to the lowest heaven.
  2. Gradual Revelation (Tanzil): Which took place over the twenty-three years of the Prophetic mission (we have explained this in detail under verse 3 of Surah al-Dukhan, Tafsir Nemouneh, vol. 21, p. 148 onwards).

Some have also said that the beginning of the Quran's revelation was on the Night of Power, not the whole of it; however, this contradicts the apparent meaning of the verse which says, "We sent it [the Quran] down on the Night of Power."

It is noteworthy that regarding the revelation of the Quran, some verses use the term "Inzal" and others use "Tanzil." It is understood from some linguistic texts that "Tanzil" is usually used when something is revealed gradually, while "Inzal" has a broader meaning that includes instantaneous revelation [[^1]]. This variation in terminology used in the Quranic verses could be an allusion to the two types of revelation mentioned above.

In the next verse, to express the greatness of the Night of Power, He says: "And what will make you know what the Night of Power is?" (وَ ما أَدْراكَ ما لَيْلَةُ الْقَدْرِ).

^1: Mufradat al-Raghib, root: n-z-l.

And immediately He says: "The Night of Power is better than a thousand months" (لَيْلَةُ الْقَدْرِ خَيْرٌ مِنْ أَلْفِ شَهْرٍ).

This expression shows that the greatness of this night is such that even the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, with his vast and extensive knowledge, was not fully aware of it before the revelation of these verses.

We know that a thousand months is more than eighty years. Truly, what a magnificent night it is, which is worth as much as a long, blessed lifetime.

In some commentaries, it is mentioned that the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "A man from the Children of Israel wore battle armor and did not take it off for a thousand months, constantly engaged in (or ready for) Jihad in the way of Allah. The companions were amazed and wished that such virtue and honor were possible for them as well. Then the above verse was revealed, stating that 'The Night of Power is better than a thousand months.'" ^1

In another Hadith, it is mentioned that the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله spoke of four people from the Children of Israel who worshipped Allah for eighty years without committing a single sin. The companions wished they could have such success; the above verse was revealed in this context. [^2]

Is the number "thousand" here for "counting" (a specific number) or for "multiplicity" (meaning many)? Some have said it is for multiplicity, meaning the value of the Night of Power is better than thousands of months. However, the narrations cited above indicate that the number is for counting, and fundamentally, a number is always for counting unless there is a clear context for multiplicity.

Then, providing more description of that great night, He adds: "The angels and the Spirit descend therein by permission of their Lord for every matter" (تَنَزَّلُ الْمَلائِكَةُ وَ الرُّوحُ فِيها بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِمْ مِنْ كُلِّ أَمْرٍ).

Given that "tanazzalu" is a present-tense verb (originally "tatanazzalu") indicating continuity, it becomes clear that the Night of Power was not exclusive to the time of the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the revelation of the Holy Quran; rather, it is a continuous matter and a recurring night that repeats every year.

Who is meant by "the Spirit" (al-Ruh)? Some have said it is "Gabriel the Trustworthy," who is also called "al-Ruh al-Amin." Others have interpreted "Spirit" to mean "Revelation," based on verse 52 of Surah al-Shura: "And thus We have revealed to you a Spirit of Our command."

Therefore, the meaning of the verse would be: "The angels descend with divine revelation regarding the determination of destinies on that night."

There is a third interpretation here which seems closest to the truth: that "the Spirit" is a magnificent creature superior to the angels. As narrated in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, someone asked him: "Is the Spirit the same as Gabriel?" The Imam عليه السلام replied:

"Gabriel is from the angels, and the Spirit is greater than the angels. Does Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, not say: 'The angels and the Spirit descend'?" ^1

This means that by the principle of juxtaposition, these two are different. Other interpretations for the word "Spirit" have been mentioned here, but since there was no evidence for them, they were omitted.

The meaning of "for every matter" (min kulli amr) is that the angels descend to decree and determine destinies and to bring every good and blessing on that night, and the purpose of their descent is to carry out these affairs.

Or it means they bring with them every good matter and every destiny and decree [^2]. Some have also said it means they descend by the command and order of Allah, but the first meaning is more appropriate.

The use of the term "their Lord" (Rabbihim), which emphasizes the issue of Lordship (Rububiyyah) and the management of the world, has a close affinity with the work of these angels, as they descend for the management and decreeing of affairs, and their work is a facet of the Lordship of the Creator.

In the final verse, He says: "It is peace until the rising of the dawn" (سَلامٌ هِيَ حَتَّى مَطْلَعِ الْفَجْرِ).

The Quran was revealed in it, its worship and vigil are equivalent to a thousand months, divine blessings and mercy descend on that night, His special mercy encompasses the servants, and the angels and the Spirit descend therein.

Therefore, it is a night of total peace, from beginning to end. Even according to some narrations, Satan is in chains on that night, and from this perspective as well, it is a safe and peaceful night.

Thus, applying the word "Salam" (Peace/Safety) to that night (instead of calling it a "peaceful" night) is actually a form of emphasis, just as we sometimes say a certain person is the "essence of justice."

Some have also said that "Salam" is applied to that night because the angels constantly greet each other or the believers, or they come to the presence of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and his infallible successor and offer greetings.

Combining these interpretations is also possible.

In any case, it is a night entirely filled with light, mercy, goodness, blessing, peace, and happiness—unparalleled in every respect.

In a Hadith, it is mentioned that Imam al-Baqir was asked: "Do you know which night is the Night of Power?" He replied:

"How could we not know, while the angels circumambulate us during it!" ^1

In the story of Abraham عليه السلام, it is mentioned that several divine angels came to him, brought him the glad tidings of a son's birth, and greeted him with "Salam" (Hud: 69). It is said that the pleasure Abraham عليه السلام derived from the greeting of these angels was not equaled by the whole world. Now, one should consider what pleasure, grace, and blessing there is when groups of angels descend on the Night of Power and greet the believers! When Abraham عليه السلام was thrown into Nimrod's fire, the angels came and greeted him, and the fire became a garden for him. Will the fire of Hell not become "coolness and peace" (bard wa salam) for the believers by the blessing of the angels' greetings on the Night of Power?

Yes, this is a sign of the greatness of the Ummah of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله, that there they descended upon the Friend (Khalil) عليه السلام, and here they descend upon this Islamic Ummah ^1.

^1: Tafsir al-Burhan, vol. 4, p. 481. [^2]: According to the first interpretation, "min" means "for" (lam), and the concept of "min kulli amr" is "for the sake of every matter." According to the second interpretation, "min" is used for accompaniment. ^1: Tafsir al-Burhan, vol. 4, p. 488 (Hadith 29). ^1: Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, vol. 32, p. 36.

Key Points

1- What matters are decreed on the Night of Power?

In response to the question of why this night is called the Night of Power (Qadr), much has been said, including:

  1. The Night of Power is so named because all the destinies (muqaddarat) of the servants for the entire year are determined on that night. Evidence for this is found in Surah al-Dukhan, which says: "Indeed, We sent it down during a blessed night. Indeed, We were to warn [mankind]. Therein is distinct every precise matter" (Dukhan: 3-4). This explanation is consistent with numerous narrations stating that on that night, the destinies of humans for one year are determined, and sustenance, lifespans, and other matters are differentiated and clarified on that blessed night. Of course, this in no way contradicts human free will and the issue of choice, because divine decree via the angels is based on the merits and worthiness of individuals, and the level of their faith, piety, and purity of intention and actions. That is, for everyone, that which they deserve is decreed—or in other words, the groundwork is laid by the person themselves. This not only does not contradict choice but is an emphasis upon it.
  2. Some have said it is called the Night of Power because it possesses great "Qadr" (status) and nobility (similar to what is in Surah al-Hajj, verse 74: "They have not appraised Allah with His true appraisal [Qadr]").
  3. It is sometimes said it is because the Quran, with all its status and value, was revealed to the Prophet of high status, by means of the angel of high status.
  4. Or that it is the night in which it was "decreed" (muqaddar) that the Quran be revealed.
  5. Or that whoever keeps vigil (ihya) on that night becomes a person of "Qadr" (status and rank).
  6. Or that on that night, so many angels descend that the earth becomes "tight" (qadr) for them, as "taqdir" also means to restrict or make tight, as in: "and he whose provision is restricted (qudira)" (Talaq: 7).

Combining all these interpretations within the broad concept of "Laylat al-Qadr" is entirely possible, although the first interpretation is the most appropriate and well-known.

2- Which night is the Night of Power?

There is no doubt that "Laylat al-Qadr" is in the month of Ramadan, as the combination of Quranic verses necessitates this meaning. On one hand, it says the Quran was revealed in the month of Ramadan (Baqarah: 185), and on the other hand, it says it was revealed on the Night of Power (the verses under discussion).

But which night of the nights of Ramadan is it? There is much discussion, and many interpretations have been given, including the 1st, 17th, 19th, 21st, 23rd, 27th, and 29th nights.

However, the popular and well-known view in narrations is that it is in the last ten days of Ramadan, specifically the 21st or 23rd night. Thus, we read in a narration that in the last ten days of the blessed month, the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله would keep vigil every night and engage in worship.

In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is stated: "The Night of Power is the 21st or 23rd night." Even when the narrator insisted on knowing which of the two it was, saying, "If I cannot worship on both nights, which one should I choose?" the Imam عليه السلام did not specify, adding: "How easy are two nights for what you seek!" ^1

However, in numerous narrations from the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, the 23rd night is emphasized more, while Sunni narrations mostly revolve around the "27th night."

In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is also reported that he said: "The determination (taqdir) is on the 19th night, the consolidation (ibram) is on the 21st night, and the final signing (imda) is on the 23rd night." [^2] In this way, the narrations are reconciled.

Nevertheless, a veil of ambiguity surrounds the Night of Power for a reason that will be mentioned later.

^1: Nur al-Thaqalayn, vol. 5, p. 625, Hadith 58. [^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, vol. 5, p. 626, Hadith 62.

3- Why is the Night of Power hidden?

Many believe that the Night of Power being hidden among the nights of the year, or among the nights of the blessed month of Ramadan, is so that people give importance to all these nights. Just as Allah has hidden His pleasure among various acts of obedience so that people turn to all acts of obedience, and has hidden His wrath among sins so they avoid them all; He has hidden His friends among the people so they respect everyone, hidden the acceptance among prayers so they turn to all prayers, hidden the Greatest Name (al-Ism al-A'zam) among His names so they honor them all, and hidden the time of death so they are prepared at all times.

This seems to be a fitting philosophy.

4- Was the Night of Power present in previous nations?

The apparent meaning of the verses of this Surah shows that the Night of Power was not exclusive to the time of the Quran's revelation and the era of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله; rather, it repeats every year until the end of the world.

The use of the present-tense verb (tanazzalu), which indicates continuity, as well as the nominal sentence "سَلامٌ هِيَ حَتَّى مَطْلَعِ الْفَجْرِ" (It is peace...), which indicates permanence, also testify to this meaning.

Furthermore, many narrations, perhaps reaching the level of tawatur (successive transmission), also confirm this.

But was it present in previous nations or not?

The explicit text of several narrations is that this is one of the divine gifts to this Ummah. As stated in a Hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Indeed, Allah has gifted my Ummah the Night of Power; He did not give it to anyone before them." ^1

In the commentary of the above verses, some narrations also indicate this point.

5- How is the Night of Power better than a thousand months?

The apparent meaning is that this night being better than a thousand months is due to the value of worship and keeping vigil on that night. The narrations regarding the virtue of Laylat al-Qadr and the virtue of worship therein, which are abundant in both Shiite and Sunni books, fully support this meaning.

In addition, the revelation of the Quran on this night, and the descent of divine blessings and mercy, cause it to be superior to and higher than a thousand months.

In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is mentioned that he said to Ali ibn Abi Hamzah al-Thumali: "Seek the virtue of the Night of Power on the 21st and 23rd nights, and perform one hundred units (rak'ahs) of prayer in each of them, and if you can, keep vigil both nights until dawn and perform a ritual bath (ghusl) on those nights."

Abu Hamzah says: I asked: "What if I cannot perform all those prayers standing?" He said: "Pray sitting." I asked: "What if I cannot?" He said: "Pray in bed, and there is no harm if you take a short nap at the beginning of the night and then engage in worship. The gates of heaven are open in the month of Ramadan, the devils are in chains, the deeds of the believers are accepted, and what a good month is the month of Ramadan!" ^1

6- Why was the Quran revealed on the Night of Power?

Since on the Night of Power the destiny of humans for one year is determined according to their merits and worthiness, one must be awake on that night, repent, engage in self-building, turn to Allah's threshold, and find greater and better worthiness for His mercy.

Yes, in the moments when our destiny is being determined, a person should not be asleep, heedless and unaware of everything, for in that case, they will have a tragic destiny! Because the Quran is a destiny-shaping book, and the path of happiness, prosperity, and guidance for humans is specified in it, it must be revealed on the Night of Power—the night of determining destinies. How beautiful is the relationship between "the Quran" and "the Night of Power," and how meaningful is the bond between the two?

7- Is the Night of Power the same in different regions?

We know that the beginning of lunar months is not the same in all lands; it is possible that in one region today is the first of the month and in another region it is the second. Therefore, the Night of Power cannot be one specific night in the year, because, for example, the 23rd night in Mecca might be the 22nd night in Iran and Iraq. Accordingly, each should naturally have its own Night of Power. Is this consistent with what is understood from verses and narrations that the Night of Power is one specific night?

The answer to this question becomes clear by noting one point:

Night is the shadow of the Earth's hemisphere that falls on the other hemisphere, and we know this shadow moves along with the Earth's rotation, completing one full cycle in twenty-four hours. Therefore, it is possible that the Night of Power is one complete cycle of night around the Earth. That is, the twenty-four-hour period of darkness that covers all points of the Earth is the Night of Power, which begins at one point and ends at another (reflect on this).

O Allah! Grant us such wakefulness and awareness that we may fully benefit from the virtue of Laylat al-Qadr. O Lord! Our eyes of hope are fixed upon Your grace; determine our destinies according to it. O God! Do not place us among the deprived of this month, for there is no deprivation greater than that.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah al-Qadr, end of the blessed month of Ramadan, 1407 AH.
